terms.def: Stochastic chain rule: df = f_x dX + ½ f_xx (dX)² + f_t dt. The (dX)² term is non-zero for Brownian motion.
(dW)² = dt in Ito calculus; Brownian motion accumulates variance at rate dt, making the second-order term significant.
Stochastic Differential Equation — an ODE with a random noise term (e.g. dS = μS dt + σS dW for GBM).
Continuous-time random walk with increments dW ~ N(0, dt); the fundamental source of randomness in SDEs.
Another name for standard Brownian motion; W_0 = 0, independent increments, continuous paths.
Derived via Ito's Lemma applied to an option on a GBM stock; V_t + ½σ²S²V_SS + rSV_S − rV = 0.

eli3: Ito's lemma is the chain rule of stochastic calculus. In ordinary calculus, if y = f(x) and x changes by dx, then dy = f'(x) dx. But when x follows a random process (Brownian motion), there's an extra term: dy = f'(x) dx + ½ f''(x) (dx)². The second-order term survives because Brownian motion increments have (dW)² = dt, not zero.

This extra term changes everything in finance. It's why the log of a stock price grows at μ - σ²/2, not μ (a fact crucial for understanding long-term portfolio returns). It's how Black and Scholes derived their option pricing formula. It's the foundation of all continuous-time finance.

Intuition for (dW)² = dt: the variance of a Brownian increment over time dt is exactly dt. In ordinary calculus, infinitesimal increments squared are negligible (dx² → 0). But Brownian motion is so rough that its squared increments are not negligible — they accumulate to a finite quantity over time.

whenWhy.title: Applying Ito's lemma: from GBM to log-normal
whenWhy.body: Given S follows GBM: dS = μS dt + σS dW. Define f(S) = ln(S). By Ito's lemma: d(ln S) = (∂ln S/∂S) dS + ½ (∂²ln S/∂S²)(dS)². Compute: ∂ln S/∂S = 1/S; ∂²ln S/∂S² = -1/S². Substitute: d(ln S) = (1/S)(μS dt + σS dW) + ½(-1/S²)(σS)² dt = μ dt + σ dW - σ²/2 dt = (μ - σ²/2) dt + σ dW. This is the log-return process: normally distributed with drift (μ - σ²/2) and diffusion σ. The -σ²/2 correction (the Jensen/Ito correction) is critical — it means the median log-return is less than the mean return μ. For μ = 10%, σ = 20%: median annual return = 10% - 2% = 8%. The -σ²/2 correction explains why volatile investments underperform less volatile ones at the same arithmetic mean return.

whenWhy.title: Ito's lemma for option pricing (Black-Scholes PDE)
whenWhy.body: Black-Scholes derived their PDE using Ito's lemma on V(S,t), the option value. Given dS = μS dt + σS dW, apply Ito's lemma to V(S,t): dV = (∂V/∂t + μS ∂V/∂S + ½σ²S² ∂²V/∂S²) dt + σS ∂V/∂S dW. Form a delta-hedged portfolio: Π = V - (∂V/∂S) × S. The dW terms cancel (this is the delta hedge). The portfolio is instantaneously riskless: dΠ = r Π dt (must earn risk-free rate by no-arbitrage). This yields the Black-Scholes PDE: ∂V/∂t + rS ∂V/∂S + ½σ²S² ∂²V/∂S² - rV = 0. Solved with boundary condition V(S,T) = max(S-K, 0) for a call, gives the Black-Scholes formula.

whenWhy.title: Ito's product rule and integration by parts
whenWhy.body: Ito's product rule: d(XY) = X dY + Y dX + dX dY. The extra term dX dY is the covariation — survives when X and Y are both driven by the same Brownian motion. For two processes: dX = a dt + b dW, dY = c dt + d dW (same W): d(XY) = X dY + Y dX + bd dt (the covariation). This is used in: (1) Stochastic integration by parts (useful for pricing claims on correlated assets); (2) Deriving dynamics of portfolio value (if portfolio has two risky assets driven by correlated Brownian motions); (3) Martingale representation — any martingale driven by Brownian motion can be written as a stochastic integral. Applications in fixed income: pricing of interest rate derivatives where the bond price and rate are both driven by Brownian motion requires Ito's product rule.

deepDiveIntro: Ito's lemma gives the stochastic differential of a smooth function applied to a diffusion process. It resembles the ordinary chain rule but adds a second-order correction term arising because the squared increment of Brownian motion equals the time increment rather than zero. For a function of one diffusion process, the change in the function equals the first derivative times the process increment plus one-half the second derivative times the squared increment of the noise. The two-dimensional version adds cross-derivative terms for two correlated processes. Time-dependent functions gain an additional partial-time-derivative term corresponding to option theta. The key arithmetic rules are that the square of a Brownian increment equals the time increment, while the product of a Brownian increment and a time increment is negligible.
formula: df = [a·f' + ½b²·f''] dt + b·f' dW

deepDive.heading: Quadratic Variation: Why (dW)² = dt
deepDive.body: The quadratic variation of Brownian motion over any finite interval equals the length of that interval — a deterministic quantity, not an approximation. This contrasts with smooth functions, whose quadratic variation is zero. Summing the squared increments of Brownian motion over a fine partition converges to the interval length both in expectation and almost surely. Because the accumulated squared noise is not negligible, the second-order term in the Taylor expansion of a function along a Brownian path survives and produces the Ito correction, explaining why the stochastic chain rule differs from the ordinary one. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ito vs. Stratonovich Integrals
deepDive.body: The Ito integral evaluates the integrand at the left endpoint of each interval, producing a non-anticipating (adapted) process that is a martingale. The Stratonovich integral uses midpoint evaluation and satisfies the ordinary chain rule without a correction term, but is not a martingale. In finance, the Ito convention is standard because trading decisions must be made without knowledge of future prices. In physics, Stratonovich is often preferred because physical noise has short-time correlations that make midpoint evaluation more natural. The two integrals differ by a drift term equal to one-half the derivative of the integrand, so results can be translated between conventions. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ito's Lemma and the Numeraire Change
deepDive.body: Choosing a different asset as the unit of account — the numeraire — changes the drift of every other asset's price process but leaves the diffusion unchanged. Girsanov's theorem formalizes this: switching from the real-world probability measure to the risk-neutral measure is equivalent to replacing the expected return with the risk-free rate, which makes discounted asset prices martingales. Ito's lemma lets you derive the dynamics of any asset expressed in units of any numeraire, which simplifies exotic derivative pricing by eliminating the need for discounting when the right numeraire is chosen. [2]

takeaways: Ito's lemma: df = f' dX + ½ f'' (dX)² because (dW)² = dt for Brownian motion (non-zero quadratic variation). [1]
Applied to ln(S): d(ln S) = (μ - σ²/2) dt + σ dW — the -σ²/2 Ito correction is crucial for long-run returns. [1]
Black-Scholes PDE derived by applying Ito's lemma to V(S,t) and forming a delta-hedged portfolio. [1]
Ito integral is non-anticipating (left-endpoint); Stratonovich uses ordinary chain rule but is anticipating. [1]
Girsanov's theorem: change from P (drift μ) to Q (drift r) for risk-neutral pricing is a numeraire change. [2]

edgeCases.q: What happens to Ito's lemma when the function is not twice differentiable?
edgeCases.a: Ito's lemma requires f to be twice continuously differentiable (C² in S). Problems arise for: (1) Option payoffs: max(S-K, 0) is not differentiable at S=K; the second derivative is a Dirac delta at K — but the integral is well-defined and Black-Scholes still works via the Feynman-Kac theorem; (2) Barrier options: payoffs that switch at barrier levels require careful treatment at the boundary (local time at the barrier contributes to the Ito correction via the Tanaka formula); (3) Lookback options: payoffs depend on running maximum max_{t≤T} S_t — handled via reflection principle of Brownian motion. The Tanaka-Meyer formula extends Ito to convex but non-smooth functions, making the local time at non-smooth points explicit.

edgeCases.q: How does Ito's lemma extend to jump processes?
edgeCases.a: For a jump-diffusion process (Merton model): dS = μS dt + σS dW + S dJ, where J is a compound Poisson process with jump sizes {Z_i}. Ito's lemma for jump processes: df = [a f' + ½ b² f''] dt + b f' dW + Σ [f(S + ΔS_i) - f(S)] dJ_i. The extra jump term accounts for discrete jumps — the function value jumps discontinuously. For ln(S): d(ln S) = (μ - σ²/2) dt + σ dW + ln(1 + ΔS/S) dJ. The jump contribution to the log-return is ln(1 + Z), not Z — important for fat-tail modelling. Merton (1976) derives a closed-form option pricing formula for this process as a sum of Black-Scholes formulas weighted by Poisson probabilities.

sources.label: Oksendal, B. — Stochastic Differential Equations (6th ed.), Springer
Shreve, S.E. — Stochastic Calculus for Finance II, Springer
